title: From local class field theory to the curve and vice versa
speaker: Laurent Fargues
abstract: I will speak about results contained in my article "$G$-torseurs en théorie de Hodge $p$-adique" linked to local class field theory. I will in particular explain the computation of the Brauer group of the curve and why its fundamental class is the one from local class field theory.

proof_stages.label: Fargues-Fontaine curve and the isocrystal-to-bundle functor
proof_stages.summary: Starting from a p-adic field E and an algebraically closed complete characteristic-p field F, the speaker recalls the schematic and adic Fargues-Fontaine curve X and the functor sending isocrystals over the unramified period field to vector bundles on X.

proof_stages.label: Classification of vector bundles and G-torsors on X
proof_stages.summary: The known classification of vector bundles by slopes is recalled and then generalized to a main theorem giving a bijection B(G) ≅ H^1_et(X,G) between Kottwitz sigma-conjugacy classes and G-torsors on X.

proof_stages.label: Torus case reduced to Brauer vanishing
proof_stages.summary: To seek a simpler proof for tori, the speaker reduces the exactness criterion for B(T) → H^1(X,T) first to H^2_et(X,T)=0 and then, via devissage, to the vanishing of H^2_et(X,G_m), equivalently the Brauer group of X.

proof_stages.label: Local class field theory kills pullbacks from Br(E)
proof_stages.summary: A key proposition proves that Br(E) → Br(X) is the zero map by using local class field theory to represent a central simple E-algebra as the endomorphism algebra of an isoclinic isocrystal, whose associated Azumaya algebra on X is an endomorphism algebra of a vector bundle.

proof_stages.label: Harder-Narasimhan filtration gives a parabolic reduction of an Azumaya algebra
proof_stages.summary: Given an Azumaya algebra A on X, its Harder-Narasimhan filtration is shown to be multiplicative, leading via the group A^×/G_m, a positive-slope subgroup, and the Killing form to a parabolic reduction of the associated PGL_n-torsor.

proof_stages.label: Unipotent devissage and descent of slope-zero Azumaya pieces
proof_stages.summary: The unipotent radical of the parabolic reduction is filtered by positive-slope vector bundles whose H^1 vanishes, so the Azumaya class is determined by its Levi quotient, which decomposes into slope-zero Azumaya algebras descending from E and hence is killed by the key proposition.

proof_stages.label: Reverse implication toward local class field theory and applications
proof_stages.summary: Conversely, the G-torsor classification together with surjectivity B(GL_n) → B(PGL_n) would imply Br(X)=0, and reversing the key proposition yields a new, albeit very complicated, proof of local class field theory; the speaker then begins applications using the Kummer sequence, Pic(X), and Brauer vanishing.

examples.name: rank-one isocrystal giving O_X(1)
examples.note: A rank-one isocrystal with Frobenius pi^{-1}sigma is used to make the isocrystal-to-vector-bundle functor concrete by producing the ample line bundle O(1) on the Fargues-Fontaine curve.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker plugs the rank-one isocrystal L with Frobenius ^{-1} into the isocrystal-to-vector-bundle construction, identifies the resulting bundle as O_X(1), and uses it to describe X as a Proj of its section algebra.

examples.name: Galois cohomology as unit-root G-isocrystals
examples.note: The embedding of H^1(E,G) into B(G) is identified, under the classification theorem, with pullback of G-torsors from Spec E to X, illustrating compatibility with local Galois cohomology.
examples.engagement_reason: Galois cohomology is identified as the subset of unit-root G-isocrystals and as pullback to X, but this is stated as a feature rather than worked through on a concrete class.

passes.reason: unit-root G-isocrystals are the ordinary slope-zero subcase identified with Galois cohomology, serving as an illustrative feature of the classification

examples.name: basic classes correspond to semistable G-torsors
examples.note: The speaker illustrates the dictionary between Kottwitz's description of B(G) and Harder-Narasimhan/Atiyah-Bott reduction by noting that a basic sigma-conjugacy class gives exactly a semistable G-torsor on X.
examples.engagement_reason: The basic/semistable correspondence is stated as an example of the Kottwitz–Harder–Narasimhan dictionary, with no verification or calculation performed for a particular class or torsor.

passes.reason: basic classes and semistable G-torsors form the standard central/slope-balanced part of the classification rather than a sporadic or counterexample case
